base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2312.16291v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2312.16291v2","title":"Observable Propagation: Uncovering Feature Vectors in Transformers","authors":["Jacob Dunefsky","Arman Cohan"],"published_date":"2023-12-26T19:00:56Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"A key goal of current mechanistic interpretability research in NLP is to find
linear features (also called "feature vectors") for transformers: directions in
activation space corresponding to concepts that are used by a given model in
its computation. Present state-of-the-art methods for finding linear features
require large amounts of labelled data -- both laborious to acquire and
computationally expensive to utilize. In this work, we introduce a novel
method, called "observable propagation" (in short: ObProp), for finding linear
features used by transformer language models in computing a given task -- using
almost no data. Our paradigm centers on the concept of "observables", linear
functionals corresponding to given tasks. We then introduce a mathematical
theory for the analysis of feature vectors, including a similarity metric
between feature vectors called the coupling coefficient which estimates the
degree to which one feature's output correlates with another's. We use ObProp
to perform extensive qualitative investigations into several tasks, including
gendered occupational bias, political party prediction, and programming
language detection. Our results suggest that ObProp surpasses traditional
approaches for finding feature vectors in the low-data regime, and that ObProp
can be used to better understand the mechanisms responsible for bias in large
language models.","github_url":"https://github.com/jacobdunefsky/ObservablePropagation","main_contributions":"The paper introduces "observable propagation" (OBPROP), a novel method for identifying feature vectors in large language models corresponding to specific tasks using minimal data. It also develops a theoretical analysis of feature vectors, including a similarity metric called the "coupling coefficient". The method is applied to investigate gendered pronoun prediction and occupational gender bias, demonstrating its ability to uncover the features responsible for these biases and outperform traditional methods in low-data regimes.","methodology":"OBPROP involves defining "observables" as linear functionals on the logits of a language model. It then uses differentiation (first-order Taylor approximations) to linearize nonlinearities like MLPs and LayerNorms within the transformer architecture. The method propagates these observables backward through the network to identify feature vectors corresponding to the task represented by the observable. A coupling coefficient is introduced to measure the similarity between feature vectors for different tasks.","experimental_setup":"The method was evaluated on tasks including gendered pronoun prediction, political party prediction, and programming language detection (C vs. Python). Experiments used the GPT-Neo-1.3B model and artificial datasets of gendered names and political figures, as well as a natural dataset of code snippets. Performance was compared against probing methods like linear/logistic regression and mean difference, with metrics such as correlation (r^2) and AUC-ROC used for validation.","limitations":"OBPROP primarily addresses the OV circuits of Transformers, potentially overlooking computations in QK circuits. The method approximates nonlinearities using first-order Taylor expansions, which may not fully capture complex non-linear behavior. Also, the theoretical analysis makes assumptions about the distribution of observables that may not always hold in practice.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions include extending OBPROP to account for QK circuits, exploring more sophisticated methods for handling nonlinearities, and investigating how OBPROP can be used to develop more powerful debiasing methods that do not compromise model performance on desired tasks. The paper also suggests integrating OBPROP with sparse autoencoders to address potential shortcomings of the latter."}
